_description: Optimal soil and environmental parameter thresholds for prescriptive agronomic intervention. All ranges are research-validated for tropical/sub-Saharan African growing conditions. Deviation logic: WARNING when outside optimal range; CRITICAL when outside critical range.
_sources: FAO Fertilizer and Plant Nutrition Bulletin No. 16
IITA Maize Production Manual (West Africa Edition)
Soil Science Society of America: Corn Production Handbook

soil_ph.description: Measure of soil acidity/alkalinity. Directly controls nutrient availability. Below 5.5, aluminum and manganese become toxic. Above 7.5, iron, manganese, zinc, and boron become unavailable.

intervention_hints.low: Apply agricultural lime (CaCO3) or dolomitic limestone to raise pH. Rate depends on current buffer pH and target pH.
intervention_hints.high: Apply elemental sulfur (S) or acidifying nitrogen fertilizers (e.g., ammonium sulfate) to lower pH.

soil_moisture.description: Volumetric water content of the topsoil (0–30 cm depth). Below field capacity causes water stress; above field capacity risks anaerobic conditions, nitrogen leaching, and root rot.

intervention_hints.low: Initiate irrigation immediately. Apply 25–30 mm of water per irrigation event. Consider mulching to reduce surface evaporation.
intervention_hints.high: Improve field drainage. Create furrow channels to divert excess water. Withhold irrigation until moisture drops below 65% VWC.

soil_temperature.description: Soil temperature at 10 cm depth. Controls seed germination rate, root activity, and microbial nutrient mineralization. Maize germination is severely inhibited below 10°C.

intervention_hints.low: Apply black plastic mulch to absorb solar radiation and raise soil temperature. Delay planting until soil warms above 15°C.
intervention_hints.high: Apply organic mulch (straw/compost) to insulate soil and reduce temperature. Ensure adequate soil moisture to buffer thermal stress.

nitrogen_ppm.description: Plant-available nitrogen in the nitrate (NO3-) and ammonium (NH4+) forms. Primary driver of vegetative growth and yield. Highly mobile; susceptible to leaching and volatilization.

intervention_hints.low: Apply urea (46-0-0) at 100–150 kg/ha split into two applications: at planting and at V6 stage. Alternatively, use CAN (27% N) at 185–220 kg/ha.
intervention_hints.high: Cease nitrogen fertilization immediately. Consider planting a nitrogen-scavenging cover crop (e.g., sorghum-sudan grass). Test for nitrate leaching risk.

phosphorus_ppm.description: Bray-1 or Mehlich-3 extractable phosphorus. Critical for root development, energy transfer (ATP), and early crop establishment. Deficiency causes distinctive purple coloration on leaves.

intervention_hints.low: Apply Triple Super Phosphate (TSP, 0-46-0) at 100–130 kg/ha, banded 5–7 cm below seed at planting. In acidic soils, correct pH first to maximize P availability.
intervention_hints.high: Cease phosphorus fertilization for at least 2 growing seasons. Excess P can precipitate micronutrients (Zn, Fe). Conduct a soil saturation ratio test.

potassium_ppm.description: Exchangeable potassium. Regulates stomatal aperture (drought tolerance), enzyme activation, and grain filling. Deficiency manifests as leaf margin scorch (firing) on lower leaves.

intervention_hints.low: Apply Muriate of Potash (MOP, 0-0-60) at 60–90 kg/ha at planting. For sandy soils with high leaching risk, split into two applications.
intervention_hints.high: Potassium excess antagonizes Mg and Ca uptake. Cease K applications. Test Ca:Mg:K ratios. Consider gypsum application to balance cation exchange sites.

electrical_conductivity.description: Measure of total soluble salt concentration in soil solution. High EC causes osmotic stress, preventing water uptake even when soil moisture is adequate ('physiological drought'). Maize is moderately sensitive to salinity.

intervention_hints.low: Low EC may indicate leached or nutrient-poor soil. Conduct full nutrient analysis. Apply balanced NPK compound fertilizer.
intervention_hints.high: Leach salts by applying 100–150 mm of excess irrigation water to flush salts below root zone. Add gypsum (CaSO4) at 2–4 t/ha to displace Na+ from exchange sites if sodium is the primary salt.

organic_matter_percent.description: Percentage of organic carbon-based compounds in the soil. Drives CEC (nutrient holding capacity), water retention, soil structure, and microbial activity. Low SOM is a fundamental soil health problem that amplifies all other deficiencies.

intervention_hints.low: Apply well-composted organic manure at 5–10 t/ha per season. Implement conservation tillage to reduce oxidation. Plant legume cover crops in the off-season. SOM recovery is a multi-season strategy.
intervention_hints.high: Very high SOM can cause waterlogging and anaerobic decomposition (producing phytotoxic compounds). Improve drainage. Consider subsoil aeration with a ripper/subsoiler.

ambient_humidity.description: Relative humidity of the air around the crop canopy. High humidity promotes fungal disease (gray leaf spot, northern corn leaf blight). Low humidity accelerates evapotranspiration and increases irrigation demand.

intervention_hints.low: Increase irrigation frequency to compensate for elevated evapotranspiration. Apply mulch to reduce soil moisture loss.
intervention_hints.high: Increase plant spacing to improve air circulation. Apply prophylactic fungicide (e.g., Mancozeb 80WP at 2.5 kg/ha) if forecast indicates prolonged high humidity. Scout for fungal lesions on lower leaves.

ambient_temperature.description: Air temperature around crop canopy. Maize photosynthesis is optimal between 25°C and 33°C. Temperatures above 35°C during pollination (silking/tasseling) cause pollen death and drastically reduce grain set.

intervention_hints.low: Cold stress at early stages delays germination and establishment. Use plastic mulch for soil warming. If frost risk exists, delay planting.
intervention_hints.high: During silking/tasseling, heat stress above 35°C is critical. Irrigate in the evening to cool canopy temperature. If planting has not yet occurred, select heat-tolerant hybrids for this environment.

composite_health_scoring.description: Weighted contribution of each parameter to the overall Field Health Score (FHS). Weights reflect relative agronomic impact on corn yield in tropical conditions. Must sum to 1.0.

EXCELLENT.label: Excellent — Optimal conditions for maximum yield

GOOD.label: Good — Minor deviations, low yield impact

POOR.label: Poor — Multiple critical parameters, high yield loss risk
